figures.caption: Fig. 16. Final reconstructed images for CC-PINN and classical cost function PINN using the frequency-hopping strategy to invert the Fresnel lowfrequency dataset FoamTwinDielTM 345 (a, c) and high-frequency dataset FoamTwinDielTM 678 (b, d). Top: CC-PINN; Bottom: classical cost function PINN. The results shown are selected from the median of the final PSNR of the 11 independent runs. Dashed lines mark the true contours of the target.

Fig. 4. Comparison of mean PSNR convergence with standard deviation band for CC-PINN and classical cost function PINN using the frequencyhopping strategy to invert “Austria” dielectric targets of different contrasts. The relative permittivity of the dielectric targets is εr =4 (a), εr =4.5 (b), εr =5 (c), εr =5.5 (d), εr =6 (e), and εr =6.5 (f). SNR= 20 dB.

Fig. 14. Comparison of mean PSNR convergence with standard deviation band and boxplots for CC-PINN and classical cost function PINN using the frequency-hopping strategy to invert the Fresnel lowfrequency dataset FoamTwinDielTM 345 (a, c) and high-frequency dataset FoamTwinDielTM 678 (b, d).

Fig. 7. Comparison of mean PSNR convergence with standard deviation band and boxplots for CC-PINN and classical cost function PINN using the frequency-hopping strategy to invert the same “Austria” dielectric target (εr =6). SNR= 10 dB (a, c); SNR= 0 dB (b, d).

Fig. 9. Comparison of mean PSNR convergence with standard deviation band for CC-PINN and classical cost function PINN using the simultaneous multi-frequency processing strategy to invert “Austria” dielectric targets of different contrasts. Target relative permittivity is εr =4 (a, c) and εr =5 (b, d). SNR= 20 dB.

Fig. 12. Reconstruction results for CC-PINN and classical cost function PINN using the frequency-hopping strategy to invert different lossy “Austria” dielectric targets. “Austria” lossy target 1: εr = 6, σ = 0.03 S/m (a–b, e–f); “Austria” lossy target 2: small cylinders εr = 6, σ = 0.05 S/m, large ring εr = 9, σ = 0.03 S/m (c–d, g–h). SNR= 20 dB. Left: relative permittivity; Right: conductivity (S/m). Top: CC-PINN; Bottom: classical cost function PINN. The results shown are selected from the median of the final PSNR of the 11 independent runs. Dashed lines mark the true contours of the target.

Fig. 10. Comparison of mean PSNR convergence with standard deviation band and boxplots for CC-PINN and classical cost function PINN using the frequency-hopping strategy to invert “Austria” lossy dielectric target 1 (εr = 6, σ = 0.03 S/m). SNR= 20 dB.
